rulings.Case Title: JOHN DOE VS. THE GREEK ORTHODOX METROPOLIS OF SAN FRANCISCO, et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
rulings.Rulings: SET FOR HEARING ON TUESDAY, NOVEMBER 27, 2007, LINE 4. DEFENDANT THE GREEK ORTHODOX METROPOLIS OF SAN FRANCISCO'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT: Defendants' motion for summary judgment is denied. Assuming that defendants shifted the burden of proof, there is a triable issue of fact as to whether Father Michael knew or had reason to know that plaintiff lacked the capacity to consent. See the Declarations of Drs. Glick and Sigal and plaintiff's response to facts 5-12. The claims are not barred by the First Amendment; rather, the central issue is plaintiff's capacity to consent; his religious beliefs are secondary. If plaintiff had the capacity to consent and Father Michael had no reason to think otherwise, plaintiff's cause of action fails regardless of plaintiff's religious beliefs. Alternatively, if plaintiff lacked the capacity to consent, it matters not what plaintiff's religious beliefs were. Defendants' motion for summary adjudication is denied. Plaintiff has presented significant disputed facts in response to Defendants' facts 21-51. With respect to the the negligence, negligence supervision and ratification causes of action, the issues include notice that Father Michael presented a risk and when the defendant Church had actual and inquiry notice. The fiduciary duty claim is not barred by the First Amendment in that this case can be tried without an inquiry into plaintiff's religious beliefs. Plaintiff's lack of capacity and vulnerability, caused by several factors, are the issues. There also are triable issues of fact as to whether the defendant Church ignored the conduct of Father Michael while he engaged in a relationship that may be found to be abusive and obviously so. =(302/PJM/PB)

rulings.Case Title: DANIEL OSMAN VS. HOWARD EDWARD SCOTT MILLER et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Ntc Of Mtn And Mtn For Change Of Venue
rulings.Rulings: SET FOR HEARING ON TUESDAY, NOVEMBER 27, 2007, LINE 8. CROSS-DEFENDANT STATE OF CALIFORNIA, DEPARTMENT OF TRANSPORATION'S MOTION For Change Of Venue: The motion is denied. It appears that the State's right to change venue controls where the State is a party named by plaintiff as a cause of the injury. State v. Superior Court (1967) 252 Cal.App.2d 637; Lieberman v. Superior Court (1984) 194 Cal.App.3d 396. But here, plaintiff does not assert that the State is the cause of the injury and in that instance, the decision in Lieberman, albeit dicta, appears to control. The State's argument that the reasoning in Kennedy/Jenks Consultants, Inc. v. Superior Court 80 Cal.App.4th 948 should apply to this case is not persuasive. In Kennedy/Jenks, there was an issue of prejudice to the cross-defendant if the public entity could maintain venue in its own county. Here, the claim against the State arises only if plaintiff prevails against the individual and there is no issue of prejudice to any of the parties by giving deference to plaintiff's choice of forum. =(302/PJM/AY)

rulings.Case Title: PIA SALK et al VS. THE REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant The Regents Of The University Of California'S Demurrer To Complaint
rulings.Rulings: SET FOR HEARING ON TUESDAY, NOVEMBER 27, 2007, LINE 10. THE DEFENDANT THE REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA'A DEMURRER TO THE COMPLAINT: The demurrer is sustained. Hearing required to determine if there is a basis for plaintiffs to amend. Plaintiffs bring this action "to declare unlawful and enjoin Defendants from permitting and carrying our research activities that violate the federal Animal Welfare Act ("AWA)." ..."Because Defendants have failed to comply with the AWA, ... Plaintiffs seek an order from this Court restraining the 'illegal expenditure of, waste of,' and 'injury to, the estate, funds, or other property' of the People of California pursuant to CCP 526a." (Complaint paragraphs 1 & 4.) There is no private right of action under the AWA (International Primate Protection League v. Institute for Behavioral Research, Inc. (4th Cir. 1986) 799F.2d 934) and although plaintiffs seek to characterize the case as something else, the remedy they seek is based on the AWA. Furthermore, the decision in Humane Society v. State Board of Equalization (2007) 152 Cal.Ap..4th 349, 356 makes clear that CCP 526 is not applicable where the challenged conduct involves the "exercise of discretion of either the legislative or executive branches of government." =(302/PJM/LB)
